title: Malignant Spinal Cord Compression
oneLiner: Oncological emergency from extradural metastasis compressing the cord; rapid recognition and high-dose dexamethasone prevent irreversible paralysis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, severe localised back pain with exquisite spinal percussion tenderness, IVDU/immunocompromise/remote infection. *Finding: high CRP/WCC and fever despite no known malignancy.*
- **Spinal haematoma** – rapid onset (<6h) of severe radicular pain then cord signs, usually on anticoagulation (warfarin/DOAC) or post-procedure. *Finding: acute, severe, radicular pain + coagulopathy (e.g. raised INR).*

**Never treat these as routine MSCC without ruling them out first.**
sections.Presentation:
- **Back pain** – thoracic (70%), progressive, worse lying down/recumbent, worse at night, often radicular band-like pain (C/Th/L dermatome)
- **Motor weakness** – leg weakness (not necessarily symmetrical), often reported as "legs giving way"; check proximal strength (hip flexion) first
- **Sensory level** – ask patient to describe a "band" or "tightness" around chest/abdomen; pinprick and light touch mapping on torso identifies vertebral level
- **Autonomic dysfunction** – urinary retention (most sensitive early sign), constipation, loss of anal tone, erectile dysfunction
- **Gait disturbance** – ataxia, foot drop, difficulty climbing stairs

**Key exam findings:**
- Assess lower limb power (MRC scale), tone, reflexes, Babinski
- Sensory level (dermatomal map from C2 downwards)
- Anal tone and perianal sensation (must do digital rectal exam – DO NOT skip)
- Post-void bladder scan (normal <100mL; >200mL implies retention)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, rigors, local spinal tenderness, raised CRP/WCC, IVDU/immunosuppression → MRI with contrast + urgent antibiotics/surgical drainage
- **Spinal haematoma** – acute onset, anticoagulation history, severe radicular pain → MRI without contrast (haemorrhage visible) → neurosurgical evacuation
- **Benign mechanical back pain** – no neurological signs, no red flags, pain better when lying flat, worse on movement
- **Cauda equina syndrome** – saddle anaesthesia, bilateral sciatica, asymmetric areflexic leg weakness, painless urinary retention → same urgency: MRI whole spine
- **Vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis** – insidious back pain, fever, raised ESR/CRP, may have paravertebral abscess → MRI with STIR sequence
- **Cord infarction** – sudden onset (seconds to minutes) of painless paraplegia, vascular risk factor (aortic surgery, emboli) → MRI DWI shows cord hyperintensity
- **Disc prolapse** – acute radiculopathy, normal bowel/bladder, usually one dermatome, MRI shows herniated disc

**Splitter:** Absence of fever + normal CRP/WCC + known cancer strongly favours MSCC over abscess. Absence of anticoagulation + gradual onset favours MSCC over haematoma.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological exam including sensory level, anal tone, bladder scan
  - Check if post-void residual >200mL (suggests cord involvement)
  - Do not delay imaging for these
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR, coagulation screen
  - **Calcium** (hypercalcaemia can mimic weakness or coexist with MSCC)
  - Bone profile (ALP, corrected calcium)
  - If unknown primary: tumour markers (PSA, CEA, CA125, etc.), serum protein electrophoresis
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: MRI whole spine (cervical to sacrum) – urgently, ideally within 24h, within 2-4h if progressive deficit** – T1, T2, STIR ± contrast
  - Second-line: CT myelogram if MRI contraindicated (MRI superior)
  - X-ray spine is **NOT** adequate – plain films miss 10-15% of MSCC cases
- **Special tests:** If uncertain diagnosis, CT-guided biopsy of vertebral lesion (after imaging)
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI whole spine** – delayed imaging risks permanent paralysis. If patient too unwell for MRI, do CT myelogram.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - **Dexamethasone** – [check local policy] e.g., 16mg loading (PO/IV), then 8mg BD or 4mg QDS. *Exception: if very high suspicion of lymphoma (e.g. young patient, B symptoms, bulky disease) – discuss with oncology before steroids, but if cord compression is definite, start steroids.*
  - Urgent referral: **Oncology SpR + Neurosurgery SpR** (+ Radiotherapy if available)
  - Consider **PPI cover** (e.g. omeprazole 20mg PO/IV OD)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS PO/IV + opioids if required (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk)
  - **Do not delay MRI** for labs or blood cultures unless unstable
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue dexamethasone with PPI cover – typically 5-7 days then taper (if surgical candidate, taper post-op)
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (bleeding surgical site, coagulopathy, high-risk for haematoma)
  - Neuro observations: 4-hourly motor, sensory, bladder scan (document)
  - Monitor for steroid side-effects: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, GI bleed
  - If bladder retention: consider intermittent catheterisation (ISL) or IDC
  - Determine histology if unknown – CT-guided biopsy of vertebral lesion (after MRI)
  - Define definitive treatment:
    - Radiotherapy (conventional 20Gy in 5# or 30Gy in 10#) – most common
    - Surgical decompression ± stabilisation – if single level compression, radioresistant tumour (e.g. renal cell, melanoma), spinal instability, or neurological deterioration during RT
    - Consider **SBRT** (stereotactic radiosurgery) for oligometastases
  - Start **bisphosphonates** if lytic lesions (e.g. zoledronic acid or denosumab) – consult oncology
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oncology follow-up; consider rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational therapy)
  - Spinal orthosis if instability (e.g. TLSO brace)
  - Bladder/bowel management: referral to urology/stoma care if chronic dysfunction
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% NaCl IV over 8h if patient NBM, otherwise oral hydration
  - Diet: as tolerated (high fibre, stool softeners if on opioids)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 + neuro observations
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH as above
  - PPI: omeprazole 20mg PO/IV OD
  - Laxatives: e.g. senna 2 tablets OD + lactulose if opioids
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Dexamethasone** – loading 10-16mg PO/IV, then 8mg BD PO/IV (or 4mg QDS). *Check local protocol for dose and taper schedule.*
- **Omeprazole** – 20mg PO/IV OD (for GI prophylaxis)
- **Enoxaparin** – 40mg SC OD (if CrCl >30mL/min). If CrCl <30mL/min: reduce to 20mg SC OD (check local BMI adjustment)
- **Paracetamol** – 1g QDS PO/IV (max 4g/day)
- **Morphine** – oral: 5-10mg 4-hourly PRN (or PCA); IV: 2-5mg PRN if severe pain
- **Senna** – 2 tablets PO OD-BD
- **Lactulose** – 10-15mL PO BD PRN
- **Bisphosphonates** – zoledronic acid 4mg IV every 3-4 weeks (check renal function and calcium)
- **Contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines, caution with anticoagulants; monitor blood glucose
  - Enoxaparin: avoid if active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, history of HIT
  - Opioids: avoid if respiratory compromise (respiratory rate <10/min); prescribe naloxone PRN
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin – if CrCl <30mL/min, reduce dose to 20mg SC OD
  - Zoledronic acid – contraindicated if CrCl <35mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Dexamethasone – abrupt withdrawal causes hypotension and adrenal crisis**; always taper over 5-14 days
  - **Enoxaparin – not adjusting for renal impairment increases bleeding risk**; check baseline eGFR
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Paraplegia (irreversible if treatment delayed >48h)
  - Cauda equina syndrome (if compression below conus)
  - Respiratory failure (cervical/high thoracic compression)
  - Steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (check BM 4-hourly)
  - GI haemorrhage (from high-dose steroids without PPI)
  - DVT/PE (prolonged immobility)
  - Spinal instability/pathological fracture (if lytic destruction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent motor and sensory loss
  - Bladder/bowel incontinence (neurogenic)
  - Chronic neuropathic pain
  - Pressure sores (immobility)
  - Osteoporosis (from steroids/bisphosphonates)
  - Psychological distress (adjustment disorder, depression)
sections.Prognosis:
- If patient can walk at diagnosis: 80-90% maintain ambulation after treatment.
- If paraplegic at diagnosis: <10% regain ambulation.
- Median survival depends on primary tumour: lung cancer 3-6 months, breast/prostate 12-24 months. Performance status and number of compression sites also critical.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always request MRI whole spine** (cervical to sacrum) – 30% of patients have multiple levels of compression; missing a second site is a common error.
- **Check serum calcium** early – hypercalcaemia can cause weakness, confusion, and polyuria, mimicking MSCC progression or causing worsening of symptoms.
- **Do a post-void bladder scan** on every patient with back pain and leg weakness – >200mL residual is an early objective sign of cord compromise, even if they say they can pee.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not withhold steroids** because you think it might be lymphoma – start dexamethasone unless discussion with oncology happens within 1 hour; lymphoma responds to steroids but the temporary risk to diagnostic yield is small compared to risk of paralysis.
- **Do not accept a CT spine as sufficient imaging** – it misses soft tissue epidural disease. MRI is mandatory.
- **Do not forget to assess anal tone and sacral sensation** – this is the only way to differentiate cord compression (U/L reflex) from cauda equina (A/L reflex); omission is dangerous.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** new or worsening leg weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control, severe unrelieved back pain, or new-onset numbness in the perineum or feet.
- **Follow-up:** oncology clinic within 1-2 weeks; radiotherapy planning appointment before discharge (if applicable). Provide 24-hour oncology hotline number.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior SpR immediately if:**
  - New or progressive motor weakness (any deterioration from baseline)
  - New inability to pass urine (post-void residual >300mL)
  - Acute back pain with new radicular features
  - Signs of spinal instability (e.g. sudden worsening with movement)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any single parameter triggering NEWS2 red
  - GCS < 15 or respiratory compromise (especially if cervical/high thoracic)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - High cervical cord compression (C3-C5) with impaired respiratory function (FVC < 15mL/kg, rising PaCO2)
  - Sepsis (suspected abscess) with shock (lactate > 4 mmol/L, hypotension despite fluids)
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest (rare in MSCC, but consider airway compromise from high cervical lesion)

rawMarkdown:
## Malignant Spinal Cord Compression
**One-liner:** Oncological emergency from extradural metastasis compressing the cord; rapid recognition and high-dose dexamethasone prevent irreversible paralysis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, severe localised back pain with exquisite spinal percussion tenderness, IVDU/immunocompromise/remote infection. *Finding: high CRP/WCC and fever despite no known malignancy.*
- **Spinal haematoma** – rapid onset (<6h) of severe radicular pain then cord signs, usually on anticoagulation (warfarin/DOAC) or post-procedure. *Finding: acute, severe, radicular pain + coagulopathy (e.g. raised INR).*

**Never treat these as routine MSCC without ruling them out first.**

### Presentation
- **Back pain** – thoracic (70%), progressive, worse lying down/recumbent, worse at night, often radicular band-like pain (C/Th/L dermatome)
- **Motor weakness** – leg weakness (not necessarily symmetrical), often reported as "legs giving way"; check proximal strength (hip flexion) first
- **Sensory level** – ask patient to describe a "band" or "tightness" around chest/abdomen; pinprick and light touch mapping on torso identifies vertebral level
- **Autonomic dysfunction** – urinary retention (most sensitive early sign), constipation, loss of anal tone, erectile dysfunction
- **Gait disturbance** – ataxia, foot drop, difficulty climbing stairs

**Key exam findings:**
- Assess lower limb power (MRC scale), tone, reflexes, Babinski
- Sensory level (dermatomal map from C2 downwards)
- Anal tone and perianal sensation (must do digital rectal exam – DO NOT skip)
- Post-void bladder scan (normal <100mL; >200mL implies retention)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Spinal epidural abscess** – fever, rigors, local spinal tenderness, raised CRP/WCC, IVDU/immunosuppression → MRI with contrast + urgent antibiotics/surgical drainage
- **Spinal haematoma** – acute onset, anticoagulation history, severe radicular pain → MRI without contrast (haemorrhage visible) → neurosurgical evacuation
- **Benign mechanical back pain** – no neurological signs, no red flags, pain better when lying flat, worse on movement
- **Cauda equina syndrome** – saddle anaesthesia, bilateral sciatica, asymmetric areflexic leg weakness, painless urinary retention → same urgency: MRI whole spine
- **Vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis** – insidious back pain, fever, raised ESR/CRP, may have paravertebral abscess → MRI with STIR sequence
- **Cord infarction** – sudden onset (seconds to minutes) of painless paraplegia, vascular risk factor (aortic surgery, emboli) → MRI DWI shows cord hyperintensity
- **Disc prolapse** – acute radiculopathy, normal bowel/bladder, usually one dermatome, MRI shows herniated disc

**Splitter:** Absence of fever + normal CRP/WCC + known cancer strongly favours MSCC over abscess. Absence of anticoagulation + gradual onset favours MSCC over haematoma.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full neurological exam including sensory level, anal tone, bladder scan
  - Check if post-void residual >200mL (suggests cord involvement)
  - Do not delay imaging for these
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, ESR, coagulation screen
  - **Calcium** (hypercalcaemia can mimic weakness or coexist with MSCC)
  - Bone profile (ALP, corrected calcium)
  - If unknown primary: tumour markers (PSA, CEA, CA125, etc.), serum protein electrophoresis
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: MRI whole spine (cervical to sacrum) – urgently, ideally within 24h, within 2-4h if progressive deficit** – T1, T2, STIR ± contrast
  - Second-line: CT myelogram if MRI contraindicated (MRI superior)
  - X-ray spine is **NOT** adequate – plain films miss 10-15% of MSCC cases
- **Special tests:** If uncertain diagnosis, CT-guided biopsy of vertebral lesion (after imaging)
- **Never-miss test:** **MRI whole spine** – delayed imaging risks permanent paralysis. If patient too unwell for MRI, do CT myelogram.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - **Dexamethasone** – [check local policy] e.g., 16mg loading (PO/IV), then 8mg BD or 4mg QDS. *Exception: if very high suspicion of lymphoma (e.g. young patient, B symptoms, bulky disease) – discuss with oncology before steroids, but if cord compression is definite, start steroids.*
  - Urgent referral: **Oncology SpR + Neurosurgery SpR** (+ Radiotherapy if available)
  - Consider **PPI cover** (e.g. omeprazole 20mg PO/IV OD)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g QDS PO/IV + opioids if required (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk)
  - **Do not delay MRI** for labs or blood cultures unless unstable
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue dexamethasone with PPI cover – typically 5-7 days then taper (if surgical candidate, taper post-op)
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) unless contraindicated (bleeding surgical site, coagulopathy, high-risk for haematoma)
  - Neuro observations: 4-hourly motor, sensory, bladder scan (document)
  - Monitor for steroid side-effects: hyperglycaemia, psychosis, GI bleed
  - If bladder retention: consider intermittent catheterisation (ISL) or IDC
  - Determine histology if unknown – CT-guided biopsy of vertebral lesion (after MRI)
  - Define definitive treatment:
    - Radiotherapy (conventional 20Gy in 5# or 30Gy in 10#) – most common
    - Surgical decompression ± stabilisation – if single level compression, radioresistant tumour (e.g. renal cell, melanoma), spinal instability, or neurological deterioration during RT
    - Consider **SBRT** (stereotactic radiosurgery) for oligometastases
  - Start **bisphosphonates** if lytic lesions (e.g. zoledronic acid or denosumab) – consult oncology
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oncology follow-up; consider rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational therapy)
  - Spinal orthosis if instability (e.g. TLSO brace)
  - Bladder/bowel management: referral to urology/stoma care if chronic dysfunction
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 1L 0.9% NaCl IV over 8h if patient NBM, otherwise oral hydration
  - Diet: as tolerated (high fibre, stool softeners if on opioids)
  - Observations: 4-hourly NEWS2 + neuro observations
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH as above
  - PPI: omeprazole 20mg PO/IV OD
  - Laxatives: e.g. senna 2 tablets OD + lactulose if opioids

### Drugs & Doses
- **Dexamethasone** – loading 10-16mg PO/IV, then 8mg BD PO/IV (or 4mg QDS). *Check local protocol for dose and taper schedule.*
- **Omeprazole** – 20mg PO/IV OD (for GI prophylaxis)
- **Enoxaparin** – 40mg SC OD (if CrCl >30mL/min). If CrCl <30mL/min: reduce to 20mg SC OD (check local BMI adjustment)
- **Paracetamol** – 1g QDS PO/IV (max 4g/day)
- **Morphine** – oral: 5-10mg 4-hourly PRN (or PCA); IV: 2-5mg PRN if severe pain
- **Senna** – 2 tablets PO OD-BD
- **Lactulose** – 10-15mL PO BD PRN
- **Bisphosphonates** – zoledronic acid 4mg IV every 3-4 weeks (check renal function and calcium)
- **Contraindications/interactions:**
  - Dexamethasone: avoid live vaccines, caution with anticoagulants; monitor blood glucose
  - Enoxaparin: avoid if active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia, history of HIT
  - Opioids: avoid if respiratory compromise (respiratory rate <10/min); prescribe naloxone PRN
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin – if CrCl <30mL/min, reduce dose to 20mg SC OD
  - Zoledronic acid – contraindicated if CrCl <35mL/min
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Dexamethasone – abrupt withdrawal causes hypotension and adrenal crisis**; always taper over 5-14 days
  - **Enoxaparin – not adjusting for renal impairment increases bleeding risk**; check baseline eGFR

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Paraplegia (irreversible if treatment delayed >48h)
  - Cauda equina syndrome (if compression below conus)
  - Respiratory failure (cervical/high thoracic compression)
  - Steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (check BM 4-hourly)
  - GI haemorrhage (from high-dose steroids without PPI)
  - DVT/PE (prolonged immobility)
  - Spinal instability/pathological fracture (if lytic destruction)
- **Chronic:**
  - Permanent motor and sensory loss
  - Bladder/bowel incontinence (neurogenic)
  - Chronic neuropathic pain
  - Pressure sores (immobility)
  - Osteoporosis (from steroids/bisphosphonates)
  - Psychological distress (adjustment disorder, depression)

### Prognosis
- If patient can walk at diagnosis: 80-90% maintain ambulation after treatment.
- If paraplegic at diagnosis: <10% regain ambulation.
- Median survival depends on primary tumour: lung cancer 3-6 months, breast/prostate 12-24 months. Performance status and number of compression sites also critical.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always request MRI whole spine** (cervical to sacrum) – 30% of patients have multiple levels of compression; missing a second site is a common error.
- **Check serum calcium** early – hypercalcaemia can cause weakness, confusion, and polyuria, mimicking MSCC progression or causing worsening of symptoms.
- **Do a post-void bladder scan** on every patient with back pain and leg weakness – >200mL residual is an early objective sign of cord compromise, even if they say they can pee.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not withhold steroids** because you think it might be lymphoma – start dexamethasone unless discussion with oncology happens within 1 hour; lymphoma responds to steroids but the temporary risk to diagnostic yield is small compared to risk of paralysis.
- **Do not accept a CT spine as sufficient imaging** – it misses soft tissue epidural disease. MRI is mandatory.
- **Do not forget to assess anal tone and sacral sensation** – this is the only way to differentiate cord compression (U/L reflex) from cauda equina (A/L reflex); omission is dangerous.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** new or worsening leg weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control, severe unrelieved back pain, or new-onset numbness in the perineum or feet.
- **Follow-up:** oncology clinic within 1-2 weeks; radiotherapy planning appointment before discharge (if applicable). Provide 24-hour oncology hotline number.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior SpR immediately if:**
  - New or progressive motor weakness (any deterioration from baseline)
  - New inability to pass urine (post-void residual >300mL)
  - Acute back pain with new radicular features
  - Signs of spinal instability (e.g. sudden worsening with movement)
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 or any single parameter triggering NEWS2 red
  - GCS < 15 or respiratory compromise (especially if cervical/high thoracic)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - High cervical cord compression (C3-C5) with impaired respiratory function (FVC < 15mL/kg, rising PaCO2)
  - Sepsis (suspected abscess) with shock (lactate > 4 mmol/L, hypotension despite fluids)
- **Crash team if:**
  - Cardiorespiratory arrest (rare in MSCC, but consider airway compromise from high cervical lesion)
